title: US Ethane Crackers Capture Middle East Polymer Outage
catalyst: The Hormuz blockade and damage to Middle East petrochemical complexes have taken roughly 46 million tonnes — about 20% — of global ethylene capacity offline, with approximately half of global polyethylene supply directly or indirectly constrained through feedstock shortages. Asian polyethylene prices have spiked 50-80% in three weeks, and LyondellBasell announced cumulative US PE price increases of $0.35/lb through May 2026.
thesis: US Gulf Coast crackers run on ethane derived from shale gas, while Middle East and Asian crackers run on naphtha derived from crude oil. With Brent elevated by the Hormuz closure and ethane structurally cheap (the US has surplus NGL production it cannot economically export fast enough), the US-Asia ethylene cash-cost spread has widened to record levels. US producers are running at 90%+ utilization, raising prices to balance the global market, and exporting half of their PE output — capturing both volume and margin simultaneously, a setup the market has seen perhaps twice in 20 years.

ticker_rationale.LYB: LyondellBasell is the world's largest licensor of polyolefin technology and the largest North American producer of polyethylene and polypropylene. Its ethane-advantaged US Gulf Coast assets produce roughly 5 million tonnes of PE annually at cost positions in the lowest global quartile, and it has already publicly led the industry with $0.35/lb in cumulative price increases — translating to roughly $4B in incremental annualized EBITDA at full pass-through.
ticker_rationale.DOW: Dow is the largest global producer of polyethylene with approximately 12 million tonnes of capacity, and approximately 40% of its ethylene production runs on US ethane feedstock — the highest absolute ethane exposure of any major producer. With its Path2Zero project in Alberta still years from completion, its existing US Gulf assets are operating in a near-perfect market: low feedstock, high product price, full utilization, and weakened global competition.
ticker_rationale.WLK: Westlake operates highly integrated US ethane-to-PVC and PE assets concentrated in the US Gulf Coast, with chlor-vinyl exposure that also benefits from the global PVC tightening as Asian capacity is bid away by feedstock shortages. Its smaller size relative to LYB and DOW means each cent of price increase moves margins disproportionately — operating leverage favors WLK in a fast-moving spread environment.

risks: Ceasefire or restored Hormuz transit could rapidly re-rate global polymer prices lower as ME exports return to market
Sustained Brent above $90 raises naphtha costs for Asian competitors but also drags ethane prices higher via NGL/crude correlation, potentially compressing the spread
Demand destruction in packaging, autos, and construction at sustained record polymer prices — buyers are already substituting and thrifting where possible
Tariff retaliation or export restrictions on US polymers (China and EU have floated investigations) could limit the ability of US producers to capture displaced demand
summary: The Hormuz blockade has disrupted ~50% of global polyethylene supply, spiking US PE prices from $0.30/lb to $0.60-0.70/lb and creating windfall margins for ethane-advantaged US crackers LyondellBasell, Dow, and Westlake running at 90%+ utilization.
body_md:
## Setup

The Iran conflict and resulting Strait of Hormuz blockade have triggered the most severe disruption to global petrochemical markets in decades. Approximately 20% of global ethylene capacity — roughly 46 million tonnes — has been taken offline through direct damage to Middle East petrochemical complexes and Hormuz-related logistics disruption [1]. The Middle East accounted for approximately 40% of global polyethylene exports in 2025, and 70% of global virgin polypropylene capacity has been affected by the conflict [2][3].

US spot polyethylene railcar prices surged from the low $0.30s/lb in January 2026 to the $0.60-$0.70s/lb range by mid-April — an approximate doubling in three months [1]. LyondellBasell led the industry with cumulative price increases of $0.35/lb through May 2026 ($0.10 in March, $0.10 in April, $0.15 in May), while Dow doubled its April increase to $0.30/lb for all HDPE, LLDPE, and LDPE grades [2][3]. Global polymer prices have risen 35-70%, with Turkey and India recording increases up to 78% [1].

## Why Now

The structural advantage for US ethane crackers has never been wider. North American PE producers use domestically produced ethane as feedstock, tied to natural gas prices that have barely moved during the crisis. Meanwhile, crude oil — which drives naphtha costs for Asian and European crackers — has roughly doubled [1]. This has blown out the US-Asia ethylene cash-cost spread to record levels, giving US Gulf Coast producers both the lowest cost position and the highest product prices simultaneously.

Dow confirmed its crackers are running at 90%+ utilization rates and will be "flat out for the rest of the year" [1]. With Middle East supply offline and Asian naphtha-based producers cutting rates due to feedstock shortages, the Americas have become the primary source of incremental PE for the global market. ICIS estimates 12-18 months for Middle East exports to recover once the Strait of Hormuz reopens, suggesting the pricing windfall could extend well into 2027 even under an optimistic resolution scenario [4].

The market has seen this setup perhaps twice in 20 years — the combination of record product prices, record-low relative feedstock costs, full utilization, and weakened global competition creates an earnings environment that consensus estimates have not fully captured.

## Company-Specific Edge

LyondellBasell (LYB) is the world's largest licensor of polyolefin technology and the largest North American producer of polyethylene and polypropylene. Its ethane-advantaged US Gulf Coast assets produce roughly 5 million tonnes of PE annually at cost positions in the lowest global quartile [2][3]. The $0.35/lb in cumulative price increases translates to roughly $4 billion in incremental annualized EBITDA at full pass-through — transformative for a company that posted a $738M net loss in 2025 at decade-low polyolefin margins [5]. At 17.9x forward P/E with a 5.97% dividend yield, LYB is priced as a distressed cyclical, not a windfall beneficiary. The C3 AI partnership for operational optimization and MoReTec-1 advanced recycling provide structural differentiation.

Dow (DOW) is the largest global producer of polyethylene with approximately 12 million tonnes of capacity, and approximately 40% of its ethylene production runs on US ethane feedstock — the highest absolute ethane exposure of any major producer [6]. The company reported a $2.63B net loss in the trailing twelve months at the cyclical trough, making it the highest-beta turnaround play in the group. The $2.80 annual dividend (10% yield) is at risk if the recovery doesn't materialize, but the Hormuz-driven pricing environment could swing Dow to substantial profitability within 1-2 quarters. At 0.4x trailing revenue and 0.93x book value, the stock offers deep value entry.

Westlake (WLK) operates the most vertically integrated ethane-to-PVC and PE assets on the US Gulf Coast, with chlor-vinyl exposure that benefits from global PVC tightening [7]. Its smaller size relative to LYB and DOW means each cent of price increase moves margins disproportionately — operating leverage is highest in a fast-moving spread environment. The Q4 2025 $1.39B restructuring charge and PEM profitability improvement plan targeting $600M in annual EBITDA improvement are being accelerated by the favorable pricing environment. At 26.6x forward P/E, WLK is the most expensive of the three on forward estimates, but those estimates likely understate the Hormuz windfall.

## Numbers That Matter

**LyondellBasell (LYB)**:
- Revenue: $30.15B (FY2025), net loss $(738)M
- Price increases: $0.35/lb cumulative through May 2026
- Implied EBITDA uplift: ~$4B annualized at full pass-through
- Valuation: 17.9x forward P/E, D/E 144%, dividend yield 5.97%
- Operating cash flow: $2.3B (FY2025) with 95% cash conversion

**Dow (DOW)**:
- Revenue: ~$40B, net loss $(2.63)B TTM
- Ethane exposure: ~40% of ethylene production on US ethane
- Valuation: 0.4x trailing revenue, 0.93x book value, 10% dividend yield
- Utilization: 90%+ and "flat out for the rest of the year"

**Westlake (WLK)**:
- Revenue: $11.17B (FY2025), decline of 10.9% YoY
- Restructuring: $1.39B charge, $600M EBITDA improvement plan
- Valuation: 26.6x forward P/E, D/E 69%, PEG 1.7x
- Edge: Vertically integrated ethane-to-PVC chain

## Bear Case

The most immediate risk is a ceasefire or restored Hormuz transit. Gulf petrochemical exports could resume within weeks of a reopening, rapidly re-rating global polymer prices lower. LYB and DOW stocks could give back the entire supply-shock premium in days on de-escalation headlines. The 3-6 month timeframe reflects the inherently uncertain duration of this opportunity.

Demand destruction is already occurring. At sustained record polymer prices, packaging companies, automotive OEMs, and construction firms are substituting, thrifting, and deferring purchases where possible. If prices remain elevated for multiple quarters, permanent demand destruction could partially offset the supply deficit.

Tariff retaliation is a material risk. China and the EU have floated investigations into US polymer exports, and any trade restrictions would limit US producers' ability to capture displaced global demand [1]. Export restrictions on US polymers would compress the domestic-international price spread.

All three companies enter this windfall from positions of financial weakness. LYB has D/E of 144% and posted a net loss in 2025. DOW has a $2.63B trailing net loss and a dividend payout that exceeds operating cash flow. WLK recorded negative ROE of -14.5%. The Hormuz windfall may provide temporary relief but does not resolve the structural overcapacity challenges in global petrochemicals.

## Conclusion

The US ethane polymer windfall thesis is a time-limited, event-driven opportunity driven by the most severe disruption to global petrochemical supply chains in decades. With ~50% of global PE supply disrupted, US ethane crackers are operating in a near-perfect market: lowest-cost feedstock, highest product prices, full utilization, and weakened competition. LYB offers the most direct exposure with the largest North American PE footprint and industry-leading price increases. DOW provides the highest beta with deep value entry metrics. WLK offers the most operating leverage on incremental price increases. The key monitoring points are Hormuz shipping status, US PE spot prices (sustained above $0.50/lb supports the thesis), and any government signals on polymer export restrictions.
invalidation: Strait of Hormuz reopens to unrestricted commercial traffic and Middle East PE exports resume at >60% of pre-conflict levels within 90 days
US PE spot prices fall below $0.45/lb for two consecutive weeks, signaling demand destruction is outpacing supply tightening
US government implements export restrictions on polyethylene or polypropylene that prevent domestic producers from capturing displaced global demand
